title: Quantum-Enhanced Zero-Error Communication and Storage under Positional Uncertainty
authors: Arnau Diebra, David González-Lociga, Mark Hillery, John Calsamiglia, Emili Bagan
abstract: Permutation channels model communication and storage scenarios in which the positional identity of the physical carriers is partially or completely lost, so that the transmitted information is only accessible up to an unknown reordering. Here we show that quantum mechanics can dramatically enhance zero-error communication through such channels. For cyclic reorderings of $n$ $d$-level systems, and in the absence of positional metadata, the number of classical zero-error messages scales asymptotically as $d^n/n$, whereas quantum protocols can fully recover the identity-channel value $d^n$. Ancilla-assisted protocols further increase this number to $d^{2n}/n$, enabling dense coding under positional uncertainty. We also analyze dihedral permutation channels and derive general Pólya-like formulas for the number of distinguishable messages in a broad class of permutation groups. Finally, for the symmetric group $S_n$, corresponding to complete scrambling of the information carriers, the number of distinguishable messages scales as $n^{d-1}$ classically, compared with $n^{d(d+1)/2-1}$ for quantum protocols and $n^{d^2-1}$ in the ancilla-assisted setting. Our results establish a fundamental quantum advantage for communication and storage under positional uncertainty.
summary: This theoretical paper analyzes quantum communication and storage capacity when the physical arrangement of information carriers is subject to unknown permutations. It demonstrates that quantum encoding techniques, utilizing principles from representation theory, allow for a dramatic enhancement in the number of reliably transmitted messages compared to classical limits. This establishes a fundamental quantum advantage for information processing in disordered or scrambled physical architectures.
main_problem: To determine the maximum number of perfectly distinguishable messages that can be communicated or stored when the positional identity of the physical carriers is lost due to unknown reordering (positional uncertainty).
main_result: Quantum protocols provide a fundamental advantage over classical methods, allowing the recovery of the full identity-channel capacity ($d^n$) for cyclic uncertainty, compared to classical scaling of $dn/n$.
method: The analysis uses group theory, specifically Burnside's Lemma and character theory, to compare the number of distinguishable states in classical versus quantum encoding schemes under the action of permutation groups.
model_or_system: The model involves information carriers (qubits or $d$-level systems) whose positions are subject to a permutation group $G$ (e.g., cyclic $C_n$, dihedral $D_n$, or symmetric $S_n$).
key_observables: The number of distinguishable messages ($N_c$ for classical, $N_q$ for quantum, $N_a$ for ancilla-assisted).
important_parameters: n (number of carriers), d (alphabet size/internal states), G (the symmetry group).
main_assumptions: The protocols assume the absence of positional metadata, and the quantum advantage relies on encoding information into coherent superpositions.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the action of group elements on site configurations (e.g., $n=4$ qubits on a ring) and show the resulting distinct classical necklace configurations.
paper_structure: The paper establishes a general framework using group theory, applies it to specific symmetries (cyclic, dihedral, symmetric groups), and derives general formulas for the quantum advantage in message capacity.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous, group-theoretic quantification of quantum advantages in communication channels that are physically realized when particle positions are scrambled, which is highly relevant for quantum memory and quantum networking.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Action of the elements of Z4 on four sites arranged on a ring.
